claim: This study investigates the effects of nitrogen on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe-28Mn-10Al-0.8C (wt%) low-density steel.
evidence_text: the effects of nitrogen on the microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C low-density steel are investigated.

claim: The LN steel has a chemical composition (wt%) of Mn 28.21, Al 9.90, C 0.82, N 0.0025, S 0.0085, and balance Fe.
The HN steel has a chemical composition (wt%) of Mn 28.10, Al 9.95, C 0.81, N 0.1326, S 0.0093, and balance Fe.
The ingots were held at 1423 K for 2 h and then multidirectionally hot forged in the temperature range of 1123–1323 K, followed by air cooling.
evidence_text: The ingots were held at 1423 K for 2 h and then multidirectionally hot forged in a temperature range of 1123–1323 K, followed by air cooling.

claim: Tensile tests were conducted at room temperature with a strain rate of 1×10^-3 s^-1 and tensile speed of 3 mm/min, yielding YS, UTS, RA, and elongation.
evidence_text: Tensile tests were conducted at room temperature using a universal material testing machine (WDW-200) at a strain rate of 1×10^-3 s^-1 and tensile speed of 3 mm min−1. The yield strength (YS), UTS, reduction of area (RA), and elongation (A) were determined

claim: Inclusion number and size were measured from 50 random SEM view fields at 500× magnification using ImageJ software.
evidence_text: 50 random view fields (500 magnification) were obtained using SEM. The number and size of the inclusions were measured using ImageJ software.

claim: Figure 1 provides the dimensions of tensile specimens (R10, Ø10, Ø16, 20, 50, 114 mm) and impact specimens (50, 45°, 10, 8, 10 mm).
Table 1 lists the chemical compositions of the LN and HN low-density steels.
evidence_text: Table 1. Chemical compositions of the investigated low-density steels (wt%). LN 28.21 9.90 0.82 0.0025 0.0085 Bal. HN 28.10 9.95 0.81 0.1326 0.0093 Bal.

claim: The number percentages (NPs) of inclusions less than 4 µm are 78% in LN and 95% in HN steel; 84% of inclusions in HN steel are smaller than 2 µm.
evidence_text: The number percentages (NPs) of inclusions less than 4 μm in size in the LN and HN steels were 78% and 95%, respectively. The NP value of inclusions (mainly AlN) less than 2 μm in size in the HN steel reached 84%.

claim: The inclusion number density is 133 mm⁻² for HN steel and 73 mm⁻² for LN steel.
evidence_text: The number densities were 133 and 73 mm⁻² for the HN and LN steels, respectively.

claim: According to Table 3, the average inclusion size is 1.521 μm in HN steel and 3.435 μm in LN steel, and the area ratio is 1.005% and 0.212%, respectively.
evidence_text: The numbers, sizes, and area ratios of the inclusions are listed in Table 3.

claim: The liquidus and solidus temperatures of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C steel are 1587 K and 1493 K, respectively, as calculated using FactSage 8.0.
evidence_text: the liquidus (1587 K) and solidus (1493 K) of the investigated steel were calculated using FactSage 8.0.

claim: In Fe–5Mn–3Al steel with less than 10 ppm N, AlN–MnS was the main inclusion, and with increasing N content AlN became the dominant inclusion, consistent with the current HN steel.
evidence_text: Alba et al. concluded that the main inclusion was AlN–MnS inclusion in Fe–5Mn–3Al steel, whereas the nitrogen content was less than 10 ppm. With an increase in N content, AlN became the dominant inclusion. The results coincided with those observed in the current work, as shown in Figure 4–7.

claim: LN steel tensile fracture exhibits a 45° shear fracture relative to load direction, while HN steel shows a crater-shaped fracture, indicating a ductile failure mode.
evidence_text: the fracture morphology of the HN steel had a crater shape, whereas the fracture section of the LN steel was 45° to the load direction.

claim: LN steel dimples were smaller and more numerous indicating severe local stress concentration, while HN steel dimples were larger, deeper, and more uniform, suggesting improved plasticity and toughness.
evidence_text: The dimples in the LN steel were smaller in size and greater in number, which indicated that the local stress concentration of the LN steel was more severe... The HN steel was larger and had a deeper depth and more uniform distribution of dimples, which suggested that the plasticity and toughness were improved.

claim: The yield strength of HN steel (647 MPa) is lower than that of LN steel (685 MPa) due to lower ferrite area fraction (1.84% vs 5.08%), since increasing ferrite content improves yield strength in austenite-based duplex steels.
evidence_text: the YS of the HN steel was slightly lower than that of the LN steel with values of 647 and 685 MPa, respectively, ... This may have resulted from the different contents of ferrite ... The area percentages of ferrite in the LN and HN steels were 5.08% and 1.84%, respectively (see Figure 3). Chen et al. concluded that the YS of steels can be improved by increasing the ferrite content in austenite-based duplex steels.

claim: Inclusions observed in dimples on fracture surfaces became the initial point of fracture.
evidence_text: Different types of inclusions were detected in the dimples on the fracture surfaces of the test samples, as shown in Figure 12, indicating that these inclusions became the initial point of fracture.

claim: Sulfide inclusions easily detach from the matrix due to weak bonding, while brittle AlN inclusions with high melting point show poor deformation ability; fine dispersed AlN refines austenite grains and improves properties, but aggregated AlN deteriorates steel properties.
evidence_text: The sulfide inclusions showed good deformation ability and were typically large; however, these sulfide inclusions could easily break away from cracks at the inclusion–matrix interface due to their weak binding force to the steel matrix. In comparison, the brittle AlN inclusions had a high melting point and exhibited poor deformation ability. For HN steel, fine and dispersed AlN inclusions could refine the austenite grains and improve their mechanical properties. However, aggregated AlN inclusions were detected in the HN steel (see Figure 12b), which could deteriorate the steel properties.
